questions.question: What is the main risk factor for endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Obesity is the main risk factor for endometrial cancer due to the increase in circulating estrogens due to peripheral aromatization of androgens in adipose tissue.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism relates obesity to endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Hyperinsulinism and insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) promote endometrial proliferation, in addition to excess estrogen due to obesity.

questions.question: What is the most common histological type of endometrial cancer associated with obesity?
questions.explanation: Endometrioid adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype and is strongly associated with obesity, as it depends on estrogen stimulation without opposition from progesterone.

questions.question: What hormone is produced in excess in adipose tissue and stimulates the endometrium?
questions.explanation: Adipose tissue contains aromatase that converts androgens to estrogens; Its excess stimulates unopposed endometrial proliferation, favoring carcinogenesis.

questions.question: Which primary prevention measure is most effective against endometrial cancer in obese women?
questions.explanation: Weight loss reduces peripheral estrogen production and improves insulin sensitivity, decreasing the risk of endometrial cancer in obese women.

questions.question: What condition is most frequently associated with type I endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Type I endometrial cancer (endometrioid) is classically associated with obesity and estrogen excess, while type II is estrogen independent and more aggressive.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of abnormal postmenopausal uterine bleeding?
questions.explanation: Endometrial atrophy is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding, due to the vascular fragility of the thinned endometrium due to a lack of estrogen.

questions.question: Which imaging study is the first choice to evaluate postmenopausal bleeding?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the initial study, measuring endometrial thickness; a thickness ≤4 mm has a high negative predictive value for endometrial cancer.

questions.question: What is the normal maximum endometrial thickness in a postmenopausal woman without bleeding?
questions.explanation: In postmenopause, an endometrial thickness ≤4 mm is considered normal; Higher values ​​require additional evaluation to rule out endometrial pathology.

questions.question: What procedure is the diagnostic gold standard in postmenopausal bleeding?
questions.explanation: Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is the gold standard, as it allows visualization of the cavity and obtaining specific histological samples of suspicious lesions.

questions.question: What histological type of endometrial cancer is most common?
questions.explanation: Endometrioid carcinoma is the most common (80% of cases), associated with hyperestrogenism and a better prognosis than non-endometrioid types.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the standard surgical treatment, allowing staging and complete resection of the primary tumor.

questions.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosis of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Endometrial biopsy is the reference procedure to confirm the diagnosis of endometrial cancer, as it allows tissue to be obtained for histopathological analysis.

questions.question: Which method is considered the gold standard for detecting endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Endometrial biopsy is the gold standard because it provides a direct histological sample of the endometrium, surpassing indirect methods such as cytology or imaging.

questions.question: What is the main indication for performing an endometrial biopsy?
questions.explanation: The main indication for endometrial biopsy is abnormal uterine bleeding, especially in postmenopausal women, to rule out endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma.

questions.question: Which diagnostic technique offers the greatest histological certainty in endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Endometrial biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard, with high sensitivity and specificity for detecting endometrial carcinoma, surpassing imaging methods.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a method of histological diagnosis of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler is an imaging method, not histological. Endometrial biopsy, curettage, and hysteroscopy with biopsy allow tissue to be obtained for analysis.

questions.question: In which patient is an endometrial biopsy most indicated as the first study?
questions.explanation: Endometrial biopsy is especially indicated in postmenopausal women with abnormal uterine bleeding, due to the high risk of endometrial cancer in this age group.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for endometrioid carcinoma type I?
questions.explanation: Endometrioid carcinoma type I is estrogen-dependent, so prolonged exposure to estrogens unopposed by progesterone is the main risk factor.

questions.question: What histological subtype characterizes type I endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Type I endometrial cancer is predominantly endometrioid, well differentiated and associated with atypical endometrial hyperplasia, unlike types II.

questions.question: What is the most common molecular alteration in endometrioid carcinoma type I?
questions.explanation: The PTEN gene mutation is the most common alteration in type I endometrioid carcinoma, present in up to 80% of cases, activating the PI3K/AKT pathway.

questions.question: What precursor lesion is typically associated with type I endometrioid carcinoma?
questions.explanation: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia is the direct precursor lesion of type I endometrioid carcinoma, with a risk of progression of 30-50% if untreated.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for early-stage type I endometrioid carcinoma?
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the surgical treatment of choice for early-stage type I endometrioid carcinoma.

questions.question: What clinical feature is most common in type I endometrioid carcinoma?
options: Severe pelvic pain with sudden onset
questions.explanation: Abnormal uterine bleeding, especially postmenopausal, is the most common clinical manifestation of type I endometrioid carcinoma, being detected in early stages.

questions.question: What is the most common histological type of endometrial cancer type II?
questions.explanation: Serous carcinoma is the most representative subtype of type II endometrial cancer, characterized by not being estrogen-dependent and presenting a worse prognosis.

questions.question: What molecular characteristic distinguishes type II endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Mutations in TP53 are very common in serous endometrial carcinoma (type II), being associated with aggressive behavior and worse prognosis.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of carcinogenesis in type II endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Type II endometrial cancer originates through an estrogen-independent pathway, unlike type I, and usually has a different genetic basis with a worse prognosis.

questions.question: What immunohistochemical marker is usually positive in serous endometrial carcinoma?
questions.explanation: Overexpression of p53 by immunohistochemistry is characteristic of endometrial serous carcinoma, reflecting the underlying TP53 mutation.

questions.question: What is the most common location of serous endometrial carcinoma?
questions.explanation: Serous endometrial carcinoma often arises from endometrial polyps, even in postmenopausal women, and may be multifocal.

questions.question: What prognostic factor worsens in type II endometrial cancer compared to type I?
questions.explanation: Type II endometrial cancer is usually diagnosed at more advanced stages, which contributes to a worse prognosis compared to type I.

questions.question: What is the first step in surgical staging of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy (with bilateral adnexectomy) constitutes the first surgical step for staging endometrial cancer, allowing pathological evaluation of the uterus.

questions.question: What nodes are routinely evaluated in lymphadenectomy for endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Systematic lymphadenectomy includes pelvic and para-aortic nodes, since lymphatic drainage from the endometrium follows the ovarian and uterine vessels to these chains.

questions.question: When is lymphadenectomy omitted in the surgical staging of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: In grade 1 endometrioid tumors with myometrial invasion of less than 50% and size <2 cm, the risk of lymph node metastasis is low, so systematic lymphadenectomy can be omitted.

questions.question: What structure is transected during radical hysterectomy for advanced endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: In type III radical hysterectomy, the uterosacral and cardinal ligaments are sectioned to resect the parametrium, widening the margin in advanced disease.

questions.question: What intraoperative finding contraindicates complete lymphadenectomy?
questions.explanation: Diffuse peritoneal metastases indicate advanced disease (stage IVB), in which case complete lymphadenectomy does not modify systemic treatment and cytoreduction is preferred.

questions.question: What additional procedure is performed in addition to hysterectomy in standard surgical staging?
questions.explanation: Bilateral adnexectomy is a routine part of the surgical staging of endometrial cancer, since the ovaries are a frequent site of metastasis and a hormonal source.

questions.question: What function does the PTEN protein have under normal conditions?
questions.explanation: PTEN is a phosphatase that dephosphorylates PIP3 to PIP2, counteracting the activation of the PI3K/AKT pathway and acting as a tumor suppressor.

questions.question: What is the main consequence of the inactivating PTEN mutation in endometrium?
questions.explanation: Loss of PTEN function eliminates inhibition on the PI3K/AKT pathway, leading to sustained cell proliferation and promoting endometrial carcinogenesis.

questions.question: What type of genetic alteration does the PTEN mutation represent in endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: PTEN is a tumor suppressor; Its inactivating mutation eliminates its antiproliferative effect, which constitutes an early event in endometrial carcinogenesis.

questions.question: With which histological subtype of endometrial cancer is PTEN mutation most frequently associated?
questions.explanation: The PTEN mutation is characteristic of type I endometrioid carcinoma, associated with hyperestrogenism and a better prognosis, unlike types II.

questions.question: Which signaling pathway is hyperactivated by the loss of PTEN in the endometrium?
questions.explanation: PTEN negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT pathway; its loss of function causes constitutive activation of AKT and mTOR, driving tumor growth.

questions.question: Which molecular finding is an early marker in endometrial hyperplasia with malignant potential?
questions.explanation: The PTEN mutation is detected in premalignant phases as atypical hyperplasia, being an early event and a risk marker for progression to endometrioid carcinoma.

questions.question: What is the most common histological type of endometrial cancer in women with Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Endometrioid carcinoma is the most common subtype in Lynch syndrome, associated with mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency.

questions.question: What molecular alteration predominates in endometrial cancer associated with Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Deficiency of the mismatch repair (MMR) system is the molecular basis of endometrial cancer in Lynch syndrome.

questions.question: What is the average age of diagnosis of endometrial cancer in women with Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Carriers of Lynch syndrome develop endometrial cancer approximately 10-20 years earlier than the general population, with an average age between 40 and 50 years.

questions.question: What screening test is recommended annually for endometrial cancer in women with Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Annual endometrial biopsy is the screening test recommended by the guidelines in women with Lynch syndrome, given their high risk of endometrial cancer.

questions.question: What is the recommended prophylactic surgery in women with Lynch syndrome who have completed parity?
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the recommended risk-reducing surgery in women with Lynch syndrome after completion of parity.

questions.question: Which DNA repair gene is associated with increased risk of endometrial cancer in Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Mutations in MSH6 confer the highest cumulative risk of endometrial cancer in Lynch syndrome, reaching up to 44% at age 70 years.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: The standard treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer is total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, which removes the uterus, cervix, tubes and ovaries, achieving adequate staging and oncological control.

questions.question: What structure is removed in bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy?
questions.explanation: Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy involves the removal of both fallopian tubes and both ovaries. This procedure is combined with total hysterectomy in the standard surgical treatment of endometrial cancer.

questions.question: What is the main objective of total hysterectomy in endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: The main objective of total hysterectomy in endometrial cancer is to completely remove the primary tumor (the endometrium and uterus), thus achieving local oncological control and allowing correct pathological staging.

questions.question: What surgical approach is preferred for hysterectomy in endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: The laparoscopic or robotic approach is preferred in hysterectomy for endometrial cancer, as it offers lower morbidity, shorter hospital stay, and adequate visualization of the abdominal cavity for complete surgical staging.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT removed in total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy?
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy removes the uterus, cervix, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. The para-aortic nodes are not routinely removed in this basic procedure, although they may be dissected in extended surgical staging.

questions.question: Which stage of endometrial cancer primarily benefits from total hysterectomy with salpingo-oophorectomy?
options: Stage I and II (limited to the uterus)
questions.explanation: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the standard treatment for stages I and II endometrial cancer, where the disease is limited to the uterus and cervix, allowing curative surgery without the need for adjuvant therapy in many cases.

questions.question: What is the FIGO stage of progestogen-candidate endometrial cancer with childbearing desire?
questions.explanation: Stage IA endometrial cancer (tumor confined to the endometrium or myometrial invasion <50%) has low risk and allows conservative hormonal therapy with progestins in patients with childbearing desire.

questions.question: What progestogen is used as first line in early endometrial cancer to preserve fertility?
questions.explanation: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (500-600 mg/day) is the most studied progestin and recommended as first line in early endometrial cancer with fertile desire, demonstrating high rates of complete remission.

questions.question: What histological finding contraindicates the use of progestogens in early endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Papillary serous carcinoma is an aggressive histological subtype, not hormone-sensitive, and contraindicates therapy with progestogens even in early stages, requiring radical surgical treatment.

questions.question: What is the expected complete remission rate with progestogens in stage IA endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Prospective studies show complete remission rates of 70-80% with progestogens in stage IA endometrioid grade 1 endometrial cancer, being the standard conservative option in patients with fertile desire.

questions.question: What follow-up is recommended during progestin therapy for endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Hysteroscopy with endometrial biopsy every 6 months is the standard of follow-up to evaluate response to progestogens, allowing remission to be confirmed or early progression to be detected.

questions.question: What happens if there is no response to progestins after 6 months in early endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: The lack of response to progestogens after 6 months is considered therapeutic failure, and radical hysterectomy with double adnexectomy is recommended to avoid progression to advanced disease.

questions.question: What structure is affected in stage IIIA endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Stage IIIA endometrial cancer is defined by invasion of the uterine serosa or adnexa (tubes and ovaries), without yet affecting distant pelvic organs.

questions.question: What is the standard surgical treatment for stage IIIA?
questions.explanation: In stage IIIA, standard surgical treatment includes total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, in addition to nodal staging, to resect disease limited to the serosal or adnexal glands.

questions.question: What imaging finding suggests stage IIIA?
questions.explanation: A nodule or thickening in the uterine serosa on MRI suggests serosal invasion, characteristic of stage IIIA, while the other findings correspond to more advanced stages.

questions.question: Which histological subtype is associated with a higher risk of stage IIIA?
questions.explanation: Papillary serous carcinoma is an aggressive subtype that frequently presents with serous or adnexal invasion (stage IIIA) at diagnosis, unlike low-grade endometrioid types.

questions.question: What is considered stage IIIA adnexal invasion?
options: Metastasis to the ovary or tube
questions.explanation: Adnexal invasion in stage IIIA implies tumor metastasis to the ovary or fallopian tube, confirmed by histopathology, without extension to lymph nodes or peritoneum.

questions.question: What adjuvant therapy is recommended after surgery in stage IIIA?
questions.explanation: For stage IIIA, standard adjuvant therapy combines platinum-taxane chemotherapy plus pelvic radiotherapy, given the high risk of locoregional and distant recurrence.

questions.question: What is the most common site of endometrial cancer recurrence?
questions.explanation: Recurrence of endometrial cancer occurs most frequently in the vagina, being the most common site of locoregional relapse.

questions.question: What type of relapse is typically associated with recurrent endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Vaginal relapse is the most common presentation in recurrent endometrial cancer, accounting for the majority of locoregional recurrences.

questions.question: What is the most common first clinical manifestation in vaginal recurrence of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Abnormal vaginal bleeding is the cardinal symptom of vaginal recurrence of endometrial cancer, due to local involvement of the vaginal mucosa.

questions.question: Which imaging study is of choice to confirm vaginal recurrence of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Pelvic MRI is the optimal imaging study to evaluate vaginal recurrence, due to its high soft tissue resolution and ability to delimit local extension.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for isolated vaginal recurrence of endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: Exclusive vaginal brachytherapy is the standard treatment for isolated vaginal recurrence, allowing high local doses with less systemic toxicity.

questions.question: What adverse prognostic factor is associated with a higher risk of vaginal recurrence in endometrial cancer?
questions.explanation: The serous histological subtype is a high risk factor that confers a greater probability of recurrence, including vaginal recurrence, due to its aggressive behavior.
